content:
# Acts Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios from Quire 86 (PDF pages 225–226) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_001_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_001_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ The first account I composed, O Theophilus, concerning all that Jesus began both to do and to teach,

² until the day on which he was taken up, after he had given commandment through the Holy Spirit to the apostles whom he had chosen;

³ to whom also he presented himself alive after his suffering by many proofs, appearing to them over forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God.

⁴ And being assembled together with them, he charged them not to depart from Jerusalem, but to wait for the promise of the Father, "which you heard from me;

⁵ for John indeed baptized with water, but you shall be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now."

⁶ So when they had come together, they asked him, "Lord, are you at this time restoring the kingdom to Israel?"

⁷ He said to them, "It is not for you to know times or seasons which the Father has set by his own authority.

⁸ But you shall receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you shall be my witnesses both in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth."

⁹ And having said these things, while they were looking on, he was lifted up, and a cloud received him from their sight.

¹⁰ And while they were gazing into heaven as he went, behold, two men stood by them in white garments,

¹¹ who also said, "Men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into heaven? This Jesus who was taken up from you into heaven shall come in the same way as you beheld him going into heaven."

¹² Then they returned to Jerusalem from the mount called Olivet, which is near Jerusalem, a Sabbath day's journey away.

¹³ And when they had entered, they went up to the upper room where they were staying: Peter and John and James and Andrew, Philip and Thomas, Bartholomew and Matthew, James the son of Alphaeus and Simon the Zealot, and Judas the son of James.

¹⁴ These all were continuing steadfastly with one accord in prayer, together with the women and Mary the mother of Jesus, and with his brothers.

¹⁵ In those days Peter stood up in the midst of the brothers — the number of persons gathered together was about a hundred and twenty — and said,

¹⁶ "Brothers, it was necessary that the Scripture be fulfilled, which the Holy Spirit spoke beforehand through the mouth of David concerning Judas, who became a guide to those who arrested Jesus,

¹⁷ for he was numbered among us and was allotted his share in this ministry.

¹⁸ (Now this man acquired a field with the wages of his unrighteousness, and falling headlong, he burst open in the middle, and all his bowels poured out.

¹⁹ And it became known to all who dwell in Jerusalem, so that field was called in their own language ⚠️ Akeldamach, that is, Field of Blood.)

²⁰ "For it is written in the book of Psalms,

'Let his habitation become desolate,
and let there be no one dwelling in it,'

and,

'Let another take his office.'

²¹ "Therefore, of the men who accompanied us during all the time that the Lord Jesus went in and went out among us,

²² beginning from the baptism of John until the day on which he was taken up from us — one of these must become a witness with us of his resurrection."

²³ And they put forward two: Joseph called Barsabbas, who was surnamed Justus, and Matthias.

²⁴ And they prayed and said, "You, Lord, knower of the hearts of all, show which one of these two you have chosen

²⁵ to take the place of this ministry and apostleship, from which Judas turned aside to go to his own place."

²⁶ And they cast lots for them, and the lot fell on Matthias, and he was numbered with the eleven apostles.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**General agreement:** All three translators are in remarkably strong alignment across Acts 1. The substance of every verse is consistent; differences are almost entirely matters of register and idiom, exactly as expected given EMET's interlinear literalism, LOGOS's formal equivalence, and PNEUMA's dynamic rendering. The Greek text of Sinaiticus is read consistently by all three, with no meaningful textual-critical divergences.

**Key decisions:**

1. **Verse 1 — "account" vs. "book" (λόγον):** EMET and LOGOS both render τὸν μὲν πρῶτον λόγον as "the first account." PNEUMA renders "my first book." The Greek λόγος is broad — it can mean "word," "account," "treatise," or "book." Since Luke is referring to his Gospel, "book" is a defensible dynamic rendering, but "account" is closer to the Greek and avoids implying a codex format anachronistic to the era. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

2. **Verse 4 — συναλιζόμενος ("being assembled together" / "eating with"):** This participle is famously ambiguous. It can derive from συναλίζω ("to assemble together") or from σύν + ἅλς ("to eat salt with," i.e., "to share a meal"). EMET: "being-assembled-together-with." LOGOS: "being assembled together with them." PNEUMA: "while he was eating with them." The Sinaiticus text reads ΣΥΝΑΛΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΣ. Both etymologies are ancient; the "eating together" reading is found in some patristic commentaries and coheres with Luke's post-resurrection meal motifs (Luke 24:41-43). However, the "assembled" sense has stronger manuscript tradition support and is the more common lexical meaning. I follow EMET and LOGOS, but this is a genuine ambiguity worth noting.

3. **Verse 5 — "with" vs. "in" (ἐν) the Holy Spirit:** EMET renders ἐν πνεύματι βαπτισθήσεσθε ἁγίῳ as "in the Holy Spirit." LOGOS and PNEUMA use "with the Holy Spirit." The Greek preposition ἐν can legitimately be translated either way in this instrumental/locative context. "With" is more natural English for baptism language; "in" preserves the spatial metaphor of immersion. I adopt "with" as the more natural English idiom while noting the Greek permits both readings.

4. **Verse 8 — "end of the earth" vs. "farthest corners":** EMET and LOGOS: "end of the earth." PNEUMA: "farthest corners of the earth." The Greek ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς is singular ("end"), not plural. PNEUMA's plural rendering, while vivid, departs from the text. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

5. **Verse 9 — "a cloud received him" vs. "a cloud took him":** EMET: "a cloud took-under him." LOGOS: "a cloud received him." PNEUMA: "a cloud took him out of their sight." The Greek ὑπέλαβεν (from ὑπολαμβάνω) means "took up from beneath" or "received." LOGOS captures this well. PNEUMA's addition "out of their sight" folds in the sense of ἀπὸ τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν αὐτῶν, which is fine dynamically but I keep the clauses distinct for precision.

6. **Verse 11 — "heaven" vs. "sky":** EMET and LOGOS use "heaven" (οὐρανός). PNEUMA uses "sky" in several instances. The Greek οὐρανός covers both, but in a theological passage about the Ascension, "heaven" is the appropriate rendering — Jesus is not merely ascending into the atmosphere but into the divine realm. I follow EMET and LOGOS consistently.

7. **Verse 15 — "brothers" vs. "believers":** EMET and LOGOS: "brothers" (ἀδελφῶν). PNEUMA: "believers." The Greek is clearly ἀδελφῶν. While these are indeed believers, the kinship language is theologically significant in Luke-Acts. I retain "brothers" as the translation of the Greek term.

8. **Verse 18 — "wages of unrighteousness" vs. "money he got for his treachery":** EMET: "wages of-unrighteousness." LOGOS: "wages of his unrighteousness." PNEUMA: "money he got for his treachery." The Greek μισθοῦ τῆς ἀδικίας is literally "wage/reward of unrighteousness." PNEUMA interprets this specifically as payment for betrayal, which is a reasonable inference but narrows the Greek. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

9. **Verse 20 — "office" vs. "overseership" vs. "position":** EMET: "overseership" (ἐπισκοπήν). LOGOS: "office." PNEUMA: "position." The Greek ἐπισκοπή (from which "bishop/episcopate" derives) means "oversight, office of oversight." LOGOS's "office" is the best balance of accuracy and readability. PNEUMA's "position" is too vague; EMET's "overseership" is awkward English. I follow LOGOS.

10. **Verse 25 — "turned aside" vs. "transgressed" vs. "abandoned":** EMET: "transgressed" (παρέβη). LOGOS: "turned aside." PNEUMA: "abandoned." The Greek παραβαίνω means "to go aside, to transgress, to deviate." LOGOS's "turned aside" captures the spatial metaphor of the Greek (going to "his own place") while preserving the moral overtone. EMET's "transgressed" is more loaded; PNEUMA's "abandoned" is interpretive. I follow LOGOS.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 4 — συναλιζόμενος: "assembled together" or "eating with"?

**The variant:** The participle συναλιζόμενος is etymologically ambiguous.

- **EMET** rendered "being-assembled-together-with [them]" (from συναλίζω, "to gather together").
- **LOGOS** rendered "being assembled together with them" (same derivation).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "while he was eating with them" (from σύν + ἅλς, "to share salt/a meal").

**SOPHIA's resolution:** I follow the majority reading "assembled together" with EMET and LOGOS. The verb συναλίζω ("to assemble") is better attested in this form than the "salt" derivation. However, PNEUMA's reading has ancient support — the Vulgate renders *convescens* ("eating together"), and the post-resurrection meal context in Luke 24:41-43 makes this plausible. This is a case of genuine linguistic ambiguity in the Greek, not a textual variant. The reader should be aware that both meanings may be intentionally present.

### Verse 19 — Akeldamach / Hakeldamach: Spelling of the Aramaic name

**The variant:** The transliteration of the Aramaic field name varies.

- **EMET** spelled it "Akeldamach."
- **LOGOS** spelled it "Hakeldamach" (with initial H).
- **PNEUMA** spelled it "Akeldamach."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Sinaiticus reads ΑΚΕΛΔΑΜΑΧ (without initial aspirate). Vaticanus has a similar spelling. The form with initial H- (Hakeldama/Hakeldamach) reflects the Aramaic חקל ("field") more accurately, but the manuscript evidence in our primary witness favors the unaspirated form. I follow Sinaiticus with EMET and PNEUMA: "Akeldamach." The verse carries a minor flag since the spelling varies across manuscripts.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
